[Richmond](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Richmond_upon_Thames "London Borough of Richmond upon Thames"), [Richmond upon Thames](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Richmond_upon_Thames "London Borough of Richmond upon Thames"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), [England](/wiki/England "England")

was a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") club formed in 1885. Based in [Old Deer Park](/wiki/Old_Deer_Park "Old Deer Park"), [Richmond-upon-Thames](/wiki/Richmond-upon-Thames "Richmond-upon-Thames"), London Welsh RFC played in the [English Premiership](/wiki/English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "English Premiership (rugby union)") in the [2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "2012–13 English Premiership (rugby union)") and [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "2014–15 English Premiership (rugby union)") seasons, after gaining promotion from the [RFU Championship](/wiki/RFU_Championship "RFU Championship") in the 2012 and 2014 play-off final. The club returned to Old Deer Park in 2015 after three seasons at the [Kassam Stadium](/wiki/Kassam_Stadium "Kassam Stadium"), [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Defunct professional rugby union club based in London, England

This article is about the defunct professional rugby club. For the separate amateur team competing currently under the same name, see [London Welsh Amateur](/wiki/London_Welsh_Amateur "London Welsh Amateur").

"London Welsh" redirects here. For the cultural centre at Gray's Inn Road, see [London Welsh Centre](/wiki/London_Welsh_Centre "London Welsh Centre").

Rugby team

**London Welsh Rugby Football Club** was a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") club formed in 1885. Based in [Old Deer Park](/wiki/Old_Deer_Park "Old Deer Park"), [Richmond-upon-Thames](/wiki/Richmond-upon-Thames "Richmond-upon-Thames"), London Welsh RFC played in the [English Premiership](/wiki/English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "English Premiership (rugby union)") in the [2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "2012–13 English Premiership (rugby union)") and [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "2014–15 English Premiership (rugby union)") seasons, after gaining promotion from the [RFU Championship](/wiki/RFU_Championship "RFU Championship") in the 2012 and 2014 play-off final. The club returned to Old Deer Park in 2015 after three seasons at the [Kassam Stadium](/wiki/Kassam_Stadium "Kassam Stadium"), [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford").

The club went into liquidation in December 2016[[1]](#cite_note-1) and was given a [temporary licence](/wiki/Phoenix_company "Phoenix company") to complete two fixtures in the Championship. Chairman at the time Bleddyn Phillips claimed to have sold the club to a California-based investment group led by Welshman Trevor Owen Shaw, but no contract or funds ever materialised.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

On 24 January 2017 it was announced that London Welsh had been removed from the [RFU Championship](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_RFU_Championship "2016–17 RFU Championship") and their results expunged. The RFU stated that their place in the league was "untenable" and the club were dissolved.[[3]](#cite_note-londonwelshliquidation2-3) The remnants of the defunct club were amalgamated into [their amateur set up](/wiki/London_Welsh_Amateur "London Welsh Amateur").

## Affiliated teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Affiliated teams")]

During professionalism, London Welsh's first-XV squad were fully professional, and they were complemented with the London Welsh Amateurs, Druids and Occies. Following the changes at the club in the 2016/17 season the club operates the 1st XV and Druids 2nd XV that form the performance playing squad, the Occies 3rd XV and the Under 23s Griffins player pathway.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

London Welsh has one of the longest-standing women's sides – LWWRFC – which celebrated thirty years of women's rugby at the club in the 2015–16 season. While still being amateur, the club has succeeded both in XVs in the winter and 7s during the summer as well as players representing England at the regional level and on the Wales national touch team. There is also a mini and junior section.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

London Welsh was formed in 1885 by and for London's [Welsh](/wiki/Wales "Wales") community, and has played senior-level rugby since then.[[4]](#cite_note-Jones3-4) Its name in [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"), is *Clwb Rygbi Cymry Llundain*.

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1960s")]

London Welsh enjoyed great success in the late 1960s. [John Dawes](/wiki/John_Dawes "John Dawes") was appointed captain, and effectively also as coach, for the 1965–66 season. He initially significantly increased fitness levels, and then led the club in an open, running, quick-passing, attacking style of rugby, including an overlapping full-back, and relatively skilled forwards. One 1968-69 performance was described by journalist John Reason[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) as "one of the most brilliant exhibitions of club football it has been my privilege to see," and by journalist Terry O'Connor[[7]](#cite_note-7) as "the finest display by a club team I can remember', further describing London Welsh 'switching attacks with speed and handling skill.'[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Seven London Welsh players were selected for the [1971 British Lions tour to New Zealand](/wiki/1971_British_Lions_tour_to_New_Zealand "1971 British Lions tour to New Zealand") (a Lions record which remains unbroken to this day).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### 2006-9

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2006-9")]

In December 2006, London Welsh revealed their ambition to leave the English league and become the fifth Welsh team in the [Celtic League](/wiki/Pro14 "Pro14"). The club later appeared to go back on this report, claiming they had been misquoted and said this would only be considered if the [English Premiership](/wiki/English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "English Premiership (rugby union)") decided to prohibit promotion/relegation, but confirmed their hopes of ground-sharing with [Brentford FC](/wiki/Brentford_FC "Brentford FC") either at their current stadium [Griffin Park](/wiki/Griffin_Park "Griffin Park") or a new 20,000 seat ground to be built at Lionel Road, near [Kew Bridge](/wiki/Kew_Bridge "Kew Bridge").

### 2009–12: Championship era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2009–12: Championship era")]

In June 2009, the club went into [administration](/wiki/Administration_(law) "Administration (law)") shortly after turning professional.[[11]](#cite_note-11) They were bought from the receivers in July 2009 by Saudex Global, owned by Neil Hollinshead, and allowed to continue in [The Championship](/wiki/RFU_Championship "RFU Championship"), albeit with a five-point deduction.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Saudex-13) According to the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") in March 2011, court documents show that Hollinshead is "alleged to have submitted forged documents and fake bank account details in order to continue his control of London Welsh and that he repeatedly lied to ensure that ownership of London Welsh was transferred over to him."[[13]](#cite_note-Saudex-13) The former shareholders of London Welsh RFC rescinded the 2009 agreement, by which they sold the shares of the club to Hollinshead, and had regained control by January 2010.[[13]](#cite_note-Saudex-13)

The 2010–11 season was the club's 125th anniversary and to kick off the celebrations they held a military tattoo on the evening of Wednesday 25 August at Old Deer Park with the Band and Corps of Drums of the Welsh Guards, plus the London Welsh Rugby Club Choir.

### 2012–15: Premiership era and relocation to Oxford

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2012–15: Premiership era and relocation to Oxford")]

On 1 June 2012, it was revealed that [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_FC "Crystal Palace FC") co-chairman [Steve Parish](/wiki/Steve_Parish_(businessman) "Steve Parish (businessman)") had approached senior figures at the club about a possible ground-share at [Selhurst Park](/wiki/Selhurst_Park "Selhurst Park"), as the club's plans to play their matches at [Kassam Stadium](/wiki/Kassam_Stadium "Kassam Stadium") in [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford") were deemed unsuitable by the [RFU](/wiki/Rugby_Football_Union "Rugby Football Union"), after securing promotion to the [English Premiership](/wiki/English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "English Premiership (rugby union)").[[14]](#cite_note-14) However a legal appeal by the club against the RFU's actions was upheld on 28 June 2012, after the appeal panel ruled that the criteria were in breach of EU and UK competition laws. Promotion was ultimately secured when it was announced that [Newcastle Falcons](/wiki/Newcastle_Falcons "Newcastle Falcons"), the club facing relegation from the Premiership, would not appeal against the ruling.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A move to the Kassam Stadium was then confirmed for the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "2012–13 English Premiership (rugby union)").

In 2013 London Welsh caused controversy by fielding an ineligible player (Tyson Keats) in nine league matches during the season, eventually receiving a 5-point deduction and £10,000 fine.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

On 14 April 2013, London Welsh were relegated from the English Premiership in their first season (pending the winners of the [RFU Championship](/wiki/RFU_Championship "RFU Championship") meeting the Premiership entry requirements) after a 14–31 defeat at home to [Northampton Saints](/wiki/Northampton_Saints "Northampton Saints"). [Newcastle Falcons](/wiki/Newcastle_Falcons "Newcastle Falcons") were eligible for promotion and therefore confirmed Welsh's relegation.

On 4 June 2014, London Welsh won promotion to the English Premiership again, defeating [Bristol Rugby](/wiki/Bristol_Rugby "Bristol Rugby") 27–8 at home and 21–20 away, 48–28 on aggregate.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
However, the club endured a difficult season back in the English top flight, and suffered defeat in all of their 22 league fixtures of the regular season, claiming only 1 bonus point throughout the entire campaign. The team therefore finished bottom and was relegated to the RFU Championship for the [2015–16](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_RFU_Championship "2015–16 RFU Championship") season. The team was also defeated in every single European Challenge Cup game, as well as every single Anglo-Welsh cup game. As a result, they became the first top-flight English side for over 10 years to suffer defeat in every single competitive match over a season.

### 2015–2017: return to Richmond and liquidation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2015–2017: return to Richmond and liquidation")]

Further information: [London Welsh Amateur](/wiki/London_Welsh_Amateur "London Welsh Amateur")

The club left Oxford and returned to Old Deer Park at the end of the [2014–15 season](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_English_Premiership_(rugby_union) "2014–15 English Premiership (rugby union)"). Following the return the club, led by head coach [Rowland Phillips](/wiki/Rowland_Phillips_(rugby) "Rowland Phillips (rugby)"), went on to win the British and Irish Cup, beating Yorkshire Carnegie 10–33. Phillips then moved on to take up a coaching role with the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union"). He was succeeded by forwards coach [James Buckland](/wiki/James_Buckland "James Buckland") who took the role of head coach, assisted by [Sonny Parker](/wiki/Sonny_Parker_(rugby_union) "Sonny Parker (rugby union)") and [Richard Tonkin](/w/index.php?title=Richard_Tonkin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Richard Tonkin (page does not exist)").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[HMRC](/wiki/HM_Revenue_and_Customs "HM Revenue and Customs") petitioned the [High Court](/wiki/High_Court "High Court") to [wind up](/wiki/Liquidation "Liquidation") the club in September 2016 due to unresolved debts. The debts were paid and the petition was dismissed by the High Court. HMRC returned to court with a second winding-up petition in October 2016 and the insolvency court granted a stay of two weeks to arrange refinancing. After failing to pay their debts, the club went into voluntary liquidation on 23 December 2016.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The club ceased to be a member of the RFU at that point and the liquidator stated that London Welsh would not be fulfilling the club's fixtures in the league. A separate entity, "Rugby 1885 Limited", was created on 21 December 2016. The club were deducted 20 points from the Championship dropping them from 5th to 12th. Rugby 1885 Limited were granted a temporary licence to complete London Welsh's two fixtures until a further decision on their future in the Championship.[[19]](#cite_note-19) When the temporary licence expired on 17 January 2017, the RFU Board met and extended a deadline to allow the new entity to show it could meet RFU regulations.[[20]](#cite_note-20) After a further deadline was not met, on 24 January 2017 it was announced by the RFU that London Welsh had been removed from the Championship and their results [expunged](/wiki/Expungement "Expungement"). The RFU stated that their place in the league was "untenable".[[3]](#cite_note-londonwelshliquidation2-3)

## Notable former players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Notable former players")]

### Players who have won international and Lions caps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Players who have won international and Lions caps")]

Over the years the club has contributed 177 players to the [Wales national team](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") and 43 players to the [British & Irish Lions](/wiki/British_%26_Irish_Lions "British & Irish Lions").

Seven London Welsh players were selected for the [1971 tour to New Zealand](/wiki/1971_British_Lions_tour_to_New_Zealand "1971 British Lions tour to New Zealand") (a Lions record which remains unbroken to this day): captain John Dawes, [J. P. R. Williams](/wiki/J._P._R._Williams "J. P. R. Williams"), [Gerald Davies](/wiki/Gerald_Davies "Gerald Davies"), [Mervyn Davies](/wiki/Mervyn_Davies_(rugby_union) "Mervyn Davies (rugby union)"), John Taylor (now Managing Director[[28]](#cite_note-28) and ITV commentator), Mike Roberts and Geoff Evans.

## London Welsh Football Club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=London_Welsh_RFC&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: London Welsh Football Club")]

The club set up an [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") side in 1890 called [London Welsh FC](/wiki/London_Welsh_F.C. "London Welsh F.C."). They continue to this day in their own right, based in [Chiswick](/wiki/Chiswick "Chiswick").
